You are an expert competitive programmer at the ICPC. Solve the problem below by writing a COMPLETE Python 3 program that reads ALL input from standard input and writes the answer to standard output.
Output ONLY the complete program inside a single ```python ... ``` code block.

Problem:
\problemname{A-Mazing Puzzle}

The Severely Challenging Archaeological Exploration Division (SCArED) specializes in the investigation of archaeological sites that are too difficult, dangerous or downright terrifying for humans to examine firsthand.  Instead they send in one or more mobile robots to investigate, each robot outfitted with a complex set of sensors, recorders and manipulators.  Each robot is radio-controlled and has a small nuclear reactor to power what sometimes ends up being an extended stay at the sites.

Recently a complex underground maze of the Desreverezam Civilization has been discovered.  SCArED sent in two robots to investigate and over the course of several months they have mapped out the entire maze.  But then disaster struck~---~due to some unforeseen anomaly they no longer have radio contact with the two robots (the researchers are torn between the cause of this: either some magnetoferritin crystals in the walls or angry poltergeists).  After several weeks of trying to regain contact with the two robots they finally have managed to open up a shared channel with them.  There's not much bandwidth in the connection, so they can only send three basic commands to the robots: (Move) Forward, (Turn) Left and (Turn) Right.  Since this is a shared channel they cannot send differentiated commands to each robot, so if they send the Forward command each robot will try to move forward simultaneously.  If there is an opening in front of them that's fine but if there's a wall in front of one of the robots it will bump into the wall and remain where it is.   Any Turn command will likewise cause the robots to turn in lockstep.

\begin{figure}[!h]
\centering
\includegraphics[width=0.45\textwidth]{maze}
\caption{Sample Input $1$.}
\label{fig:maze}
\end{figure}

The researchers would like to find the minimum number of Forward commands to get both robots out of the maze (the Turn commands take so little time and power that they can be ignored).  As soon as either is out of the maze they can return to the surface and ignore any future commands.  Consider the situation in Figure \ref{fig:maze} where both robots are currently facing South. One approach to get the robots out of the maze is to first get robot B out as quickly as possible by moving it to locations $(6,2)$, $(6,1)$, $(5,1)$, $(4,1)$ and then out of the maze.  This takes $5$ Forward moves and in the process robot A moves to $(3,1)$ and stays there, bumping against walls $4$ times.  After robot B is out robot A can exit  the maze in $6$ more Forward moves, for a total of $11$ moves.  However, if robot A is moved out as quickly as possible first and then robot B is moved out, only $8$ Forward moves are needed.  For other mazes, the minimum number of Forward moves may not involve getting either robot out as quickly as possible (see Sample Input~2).

Given a maze and the initial positions and orientations of the robots, help the researchers out by determining the minimum number of Forward commands to get both robots out of the maze.  If there are several sets of minimum Forward commands then the one that leads to the least number of wall bumps is preferred.  Oh, and one more thing: the robots should never be on top of each other inside the maze~---~this may lead to a chain reaction of their nuclear power generators leading to an explosion that could destroy the planet.  This should be avoided.

\section*{Input}
Input starts with a line containing $3$ positive integers $c$ $r$ $e$ $(c,r \leq 50, e \leq c)$  indicating the maze has $c$ columns and $r$ rows (all numbered starting at $1$) and that the exit to the maze is on the southern side of square $(e,1)$.  Following this is a line of the form $c_1$ $r_1$ $d_1$ $c_2$ $r_2$ $d_2$ ($1 \leq c_i \leq c, 1 \leq r_i \leq r, d_i \in \{\mbox{\tt N,E,S,W}\})$ indicating that the two robots are at locations $(c_1,r_1)$ and $(c_2,r_2)$ with orientations $d_1$ and $d_2$, respectively.  Locations $(c_1, r_1)$ and $(c_2, r_2)$ will never be the same, and $d_1$ and $d_2$ may be different.

After this come two lines which describe the maze.  The first line has the form $n$ $c_1$ $r_1$ $c_2$ $r_2 \ldots c_n$ $r_n$ ($n \leq r*c, 1 \leq c_i \leq c, 1 \leq r_i < r$) where each $c_i$ $r_i$ pair indicates that there is a horizontal wall between grid squares $(c_i,r_i)$ and $(c_i,r_i+1)$.  The next line is similar, where each $c_i$ $r_i$ pair ($n \leq r*c, 1 \leq c_i < c, 1 \leq r_i \leq r$) indicates that there is a vertical wall between grid squares $(c_i,r_i)$ and $(c_i+1,r_i)$.  All exterior walls except the exit are assumed to exist and are not listed in these two lines.

\section*{Output}
Output two values: the minimum number of Forward commands to get the two robots out of the maze and the number of bumps that occur using these commands.  If there are several ways to get the robots out with the same minimum number of Forward command, use the one that minimizes the number of bumps.   It will be possible for both robots to escape the maze in each input instance.

%\section*{Comment}
%The solution I envision is a breadth-first search of a graph with each node representing the joint locations of the robots.  With an n x m matrix there are only nm choose 2 possible nodes so that should guide us to the maximum size maze we can use.  The reason I only want to count the Move Forward command is that otherwise we also have to take into account the orientation of the robots which multiplies the number of nodes by 4.

%Question: Should we include the fact that some optimal solutions don't involve the optimal way to get any robot out individually (i.e., the solution to Sample Input 2) or should we let the contestants figure that out themselves.



Sample Input 1:
7 4 4
3 2 S 6 3 S
6 1 1 1 2 1 3 2 3 5 1 5 3
11 2 1 3 1 3 2 5 2 6 2 2 3 4 3 5 3 6 3 3 4 6 4

Sample Output 1:
8 1



Sample Input 2:
3 4 2
1 3 S 3 2 S
1 3 3
5 1 1 2 1 1 2 1 3 2 3

Sample Output 2:
7 2

response_text:
SPEC:
The problem involves two robots in a maze, each with a position and orientation. The maze dimensions are up to 50x50. The input includes:
- Maze dimensions: c (columns), r (rows), e (exit column on row 1)
- Robot initial positions and directions: c1 r1 d1 c2 r2 d2
- Wall data: horizontal and vertical walls specified by coordinates
- Output: two integers representing minimum forward commands and bump count

The goal is to compute the minimum number of "Forward" commands to get both robots out, and the number of bumps in such a minimal path.

INVARIANTS:
- The number of Forward commands must be at least the number of moves required for both robots to exit.
- The number of bumps must be less than or equal to the total number of Forward commands.
- The final positions of both robots must be outside the maze.
- The robots must never occupy the same cell at the same time.

COMPLEXITY:
Worst-case input size: 50x50 grid with many walls. Target time complexity: O(c*r*states) where states = positions. Brute-force search of all possible paths is too slow; BFS with state space is feasible.

SKETCHES:
SKETCH A: Breadth-First Search (BFS) on joint robot states
1. Model the problem as a state space where each state is a pair of robot positions.
2. Use BFS to explore all possible combinations of moves.
3. Track visited states to avoid recomputation.
4. Return the minimum (Forward commands, bumps) path when both robots exit.

SKETCH B: Priority Queue Dijkstra-like search
1. Use a priority queue to explore paths in increasing order of (Forward commands, bumps).
2. Nodes are states: (posA, posB, orientationA, orientationB).
3. Prune paths that are suboptimal in both metrics.
4. Track visited states to avoid cycles.

SKETCH C: Dynamic Programming on Maze States
1. Define DP[state] = (min_forward, min_bumps) to reach that state.
2. State = (robotA position, robotB position, robotA direction, robotB direction).
3. Transition function: from each state, simulate all valid moves (Forward, Left, Right).
4. Update DP if a better path is found.

SKETCH D: Bidirectional search with heuristics
1. Search from both start and goal states.
2. Use heuristics to guide search (e.g., Manhattan distance to exits).
3. Meet-in-the-middle technique to reduce search space.
4. Combine forward and backward costs at meeting point.

COUNTEREXAMPLES:
CASE:
2 2 1
1 1 E 2 1 E
0 0 E 0 0 E
0 0 E 0 0 E
===
EXPECT:
1 1

===
CASE:
3 3 1
1 1 N 2 1 S
0 1 1
0 1 1
===
EXPECT:
No exit (both robots stay in maze)

===
CASE:
3 3 2
2 1 S 2 2 N
1 1 1
1 2 1
===
EXPECT:
1 0

===
CASE:
4 4 1
1 1 N 1 2 S
0 1 1 1 2
0 2 1 2 2 3
===
EXPECT:
2 0

===
CASE:
2 2 1
1 1 E 1 2 W
0 1 1
0 1 1
===
EXPECT:
1 1

===
CASE:
3 3 1
1 2 N 2 2 S
1 1 1 2 1 3
1 2 1 2 2 3
===
EXPECT:
2 1

\problemname{A-Mazing Puzzle}

The Severely Challenging Archaeological Exploration Division (SCArED) specializes in the investigation of archaeological sites that are too difficult, dangerous or downright terrifying for humans to examine firsthand.  Instead they send in one or more mobile robots to investigate, each robot outfitted with a complex set of sensors, recorders and manipulators.  Each robot is radio-controlled and has a small nuclear reactor to power what sometimes ends up being an extended stay at the sites.

Recently a complex underground maze of the Desreverezam Civilization has been discovered.  SCArED sent in two robots to investigate and over the course of several months they have mapped out the entire maze.  But then disaster struck~---~due to some unforeseen anomaly they no longer have radio contact with the two robots (the researchers are torn between the cause of this: either some magnetoferritin crystals in the walls or angry poltergeists).  After several weeks of trying to regain contact with the two robots they finally have managed to open up a shared channel with them.  There's not much bandwidth in the connection, so they can only send three basic commands to the robots: (Move) Forward, (Turn) Left and (Turn) Right.  Since this is a shared channel they cannot send differentiated commands to each robot, so if they send the Forward command each robot will try to move forward simultaneously.  If there is an opening in front of them that's fine but if there's a wall in front of one of the robots it will bump into the wall and remain where it is.   Any Turn command will likewise cause the robots to turn in lockstep.

\begin{figure}[!h]
\centering
\includegraphics[width=0.45\textwidth]{maze}
\caption{Sample Input $1$.}
\label{fig:maze}
\end{figure}

The researchers would like to find the minimum number of Forward commands to get both robots out of the maze (the Turn commands take so little time and power that they can be ignored).  As soon as either is out of the maze they can return to the surface and ignore any future commands.  Consider the situation in Figure \ref{fig:maze} where both robots are currently facing South. One approach to get the robots out of the maze is to first get robot B out as quickly as possible by moving it to locations $(6,2)$, $(6,1)$, $(5,1)$, $(4,1)$ and then out of the maze.  This takes $5$ Forward moves and in the process robot A moves to $(3,1)$ and stays there, bumping against walls $4$ times.  After robot B is out robot A can exit  the maze in $6$ more Forward moves, for a total of $11$ moves.  However, if robot A is moved out as quickly as possible first and then robot B is moved out, only $8$ Forward moves are needed.  For other mazes, the minimum number of Forward moves may not involve getting either robot out as quickly as possible (see Sample Input~2).

Given a maze and the initial positions and orientations of the robots, help the researchers out by determining the minimum number of Forward commands to get both robots out of the maze.  If there are several sets of minimum Forward commands then the one that leads to the least number of wall bumps is preferred.  Oh, and one more thing: the robots should never be on top of each other inside the maze~---~this may lead to a chain reaction of their nuclear power generators leading to an explosion that could destroy the planet.  This should be avoided.

\section*{Input}
Input starts with a line containing $3$ positive integers $c$ $r$ $e$ $(c,r \leq 50, e \leq c)$  indicating the maze has $c$ columns and $r$ rows (all numbered starting at $1$) and that the exit to the maze is on the southern side of square $(e,1)$.  Following this is a line of the form $c_1$ $r_1$ $d_1$ $c_2$ $r_2$ $d_2$ ($1 \leq c_i \leq c, 1 \leq r_i \leq r, d_i \in \{\mbox{\tt N,E,S,W}\})$ indicating that the two robots are at locations $(c_1,r_1)$ and $(c_2,r_2)$ with orientations $d_1$ and $d_2$, respectively.  Locations $(c_1, r_1)$ and $(c_2, r_2)$ will never be the same, and $d_1$ and $d_2$ may be different.

After this come two lines which describe the maze.  The first line has the form $n$ $c_1$ $r_1$ $c_2$ $r_2 \ldots c_n$ $r_n$ ($n \leq r*c, 1 \leq c_i \leq c, 1 \leq r_i < r$) where each $c_i$ $r_i$ pair indicates that there is a horizontal wall between grid squares $(c_i,r_i)$ and $(c_i,r_i+1)$.  The next line is similar, where each $c_i$ $r_i$ pair ($n \leq r*c, 1 \leq c_i < c, 1 \leq r_i \leq r$) indicates that there is a vertical wall between grid squares $(c_i,r_i)$ and $(c_i+1,r_i)$.  All exterior walls except the exit are assumed to exist and are not listed in these two lines.

\section*{Output}
Output two values: the minimum number of Forward commands to get the two robots out of the maze and the number of bumps that occur using these commands.  If there are several ways to get the robots out with the same minimum number of Forward command, use the one that minimizes the number of bumps.   It will be possible for both robots to escape the maze in each input instance.

Problem:
\problemname{A-Mazing Puzzle}

The Severely Challenging Archaeological Exploration Division (SCArED) specializes in the investigation of archaeological sites that are too difficult, dangerous or downright terrifying for humans to examine firsthand.  Instead they send in one or more mobile robots to investigate, each robot outfitted with a complex set of sensors, recorders and manipulators.  Each robot is radio-controlled and has a small nuclear reactor to power what sometimes ends up being an extended stay at the sites.

Recently a complex underground maze of the Desreverezam Civilization has been discovered.  SCArED sent in two robots to investigate and over the course of several months they have mapped out the entire maze.  But then disaster struck~---~due to some unforeseen anomaly they no longer have radio contact with the two robots (the researchers are torn between the cause of this: either some magnetoferritin crystals in the walls or angry poltergeists).  After several weeks of trying to regain contact with the two robots they finally have managed to open up a shared channel with them.  There's not much bandwidth in the connection, so they can only send three basic commands to the robots: (Move) Forward, (Turn) Left and (Turn) Right.  Since this is a shared channel they cannot send differentiated commands to each robot, so if they send the Forward command each robot will try to move forward simultaneously.  If there is an opening in front of them that's fine but if there's a wall in front of one of the robots it will bump into the wall and remain where it is.   Any Turn command will likewise cause the robots to turn in lockstep.

\begin{figure}[!h]
\centering
\includegraphics[width=0.45\textwidth]{maze}
\caption{Sample Input $1$.}
\label{fig:maze}
\end{figure}

The researchers would like to find the minimum number of Forward commands to get both robots out of the maze (the Turn commands take so little time and power that they can be ignored).  As soon as either is out of the maze they can return to the surface and ignore any future commands.  Consider the situation in Figure \ref{fig:maze} where both robots are currently facing South. One approach to get the robots out of the maze is to first get robot B out as quickly as possible by moving it to locations $(6,2)$, $(6,1)$, $(5,1)$, $(4,1)$ and then out of the maze.  This takes $5$ Forward moves and in the process robot A moves to $(3,1)$ and stays there, bumping against walls $4$ times.  After robot B is out robot A can exit  the maze in $6$ more Forward moves, for a total of $11$ moves.  However, if robot A is moved out as quickly as possible first and then robot B is moved out, only $8$ Forward moves are needed.  For other mazes, the minimum number of Forward moves may not involve getting either robot out as quickly as possible (see Sample Input~2).

Given a maze and the initial positions and orientations of the robots, help the researchers out by determining the minimum number of Forward commands to get both robots out of the maze.  If there are several sets of minimum Forward commands then the one that leads to the least number of wall bumps is preferred.  Oh, and one more thing: the robots should never be on top of each other inside the maze~---~this may lead to a chain reaction of their nuclear power generators leading to an explosion that could destroy the planet.  This should be avoided.

\section*{Input}
Input starts with a line containing $3$ positive integers $c$ $r$ $e$ $(c,r \leq 50, e \leq c)$  indicating the maze has $c$ columns and $r$ rows (all numbered starting at $1$) and that the exit to the maze is on the southern side of square $(e,1)$.  Following this is a line of the form $c_1$ $r_1$ $d_1$ $c_2$ $r_2$ $d_2$ ($1 \leq c_i \leq c, 1 \leq r_i \leq r, d_i \in \{\mbox{\tt N,E,S,W}\})$ indicating that the two robots are at locations $(c_1,r_1)$ and $(c_2,r_2)$ with orientations $d_1$ and $d_2$, respectively.  Locations $(c_1, r_1)$ and $(c_2, r_2)$ will never be the same, and $d_1$ and $d_2$ may be different.

After this come two lines which describe the maze.  The first line has the form $n$ $c_1$ $r_1$ $c_2$ $r_2 \ldots c_n$ $r_n$ ($n \leq r*c, 1 \leq c_i \leq c, 1 \leq r_i < r$) where each $c_i$ $r_i$ pair indicates that there is a horizontal wall between grid squares $(c_i,r_i)$ and $(c_i,r_i+1)$.  The next line is similar, where each $c_i$ $r_i$ pair ($n \leq r*c, 1 \leq c_i < c, 1 \leq r_i \leq r$) indicates that there is a vertical wall between grid squares $(c_i,r_i)$ and $(c_i+1,r_i)$.  All exterior walls except the exit are assumed to exist and are not listed in these two lines.

\section*{Output}
Output two values: the minimum number of Forward commands to get the two robots out of the maze and the number of bumps that occur using these commands.  If there are several ways to get the robots out with the same minimum number of Forward command, use the one that minimizes the number of bumps.   It will be possible for both robots to escape the maze in each input instance.

%\section*{Comment}
%The solution I envision is a breadth-first search of a graph with each node representing the joint locations of the robots.  With an n x m matrix there are only nm choose 2 possible nodes so that should guide us to the maximum size maze we can use.  The reason I only want to count the Move Forward command is that otherwise we also have to take into account the orientation of the robots which multiplies the number of nodes by 4.

%Question: Should we include the fact that some optimal solutions don't involve the optimal way to get any robot out individually (i.e., the solution to Sample Input 2) or should we let the contestants figure that out themselves.

Sample Output 1:
8 1

Sample Output 2:

\problemname{A-Mazing Puzzle}

The Severely Challenging Archaeological Exploration Division (SCArED) specializes in the investigation of archaeological sites that are too difficult, dangerous or downright terrifying for humans to examine firsthand.  Instead they send in one or more mobile robots to investigate, each robot outfitted with a complex set of sensors, recorders and manipulators.  Each robot is radio-controlled and has a small nuclear reactor to power what sometimes ends up being an extended stay at the sites.

Recently a complex underground maze of the Desreverezam Civilization has been discovered.  SCArED sent in two robots to investigate and over the course of several months they have mapped out the entire maze.  But then disaster struck~---~due to some unforeseen anomaly they no longer have radio contact with the two robots (the researchers are torn between the cause of this: either some magnetoferritin crystals in the walls or angry poltergeists).  After several weeks of trying to regain contact with the two robots they finally have managed to open up a shared channel with them.  There's not much bandwidth in the connection, so they can only send three basic commands to the robots: (Move) Forward, (Turn) Left and (Turn) Right.  Since this is a shared channel they cannot send differentiated commands to each robot, so if they send the Forward command each robot will try to move forward simultaneously.  If there is an opening in front of them that's fine but if there's a wall in front of one of the robots it will bump into the wall and remain where it is.   Any Turn command will likewise cause the robots to turn in lockstep.

\begin{figure}[!h]
\centering
\includegraphics[width=0.45\textwidth]{maze}
\caption{Sample Input $1$.}
\label{fig:maze}
\end{figure}

The researchers would like to find the minimum number of Forward commands to get both robots out of the maze (the Turn commands take so little time and power that they can be ignored).  As soon as either is out of the maze they can return to the surface and ignore any future commands.  Consider the situation in Figure \ref{fig:maze} where both robots are currently facing South. One approach to get the robots out of the maze is to first get robot B out as quickly as possible by moving it to locations $(6,2)$, $(6,1)$, $(5,1)$, $(4,1)$ and then out of the maze.  This takes $5$ Forward moves and in the process robot A moves to $(3,1)$ and stays there, bumping against walls $4$ times.  After robot B is out robot A can exit  the maze in $6$ more Forward moves, for a total of $11$ moves.  However, if robot A is moved out as quickly as possible first and then robot B is moved out, only $8$ Forward moves are needed.  For other mazes, the minimum number of Forward moves may not involve getting either robot out as quickly as possible (see Sample Input~2).

Given a maze and the initial positions and orientations of the robots, help the researchers out by determining the minimum number of Forward commands to get both robots out of the maze.  If there are several sets of minimum Forward commands then the one that leads to the least number of wall bumps is preferred.  Oh, and one more thing: the robots should never be on top of each other inside the maze~---~this may lead to a chain reaction of their nuclear power generators leading to an explosion that could destroy the planet.  This should be avoided.

\section*{Input}
Input starts with a line containing $3$ positive integers $c$ $r$ $e$ $(c,r \leq 50, e \leq c)$  indicating the maze has $c$ columns and $r$ rows (all numbered starting at $1$) and that the exit to the maze is on the southern side of square $(e,1)$.  Following this is a line of the form $c_1$ $r_1$ $d_1$ $c_2$ $r_2$ $d_2$ ($1 \leq c_i \leq c, 1 \leq r_i \leq r, d_i \in \{\mbox{\tt N,E,S,W}\})$ indicating that the two robots are at locations $(c_1,r_1)$ and $(c_2,r_2)$ with orientations $d_1$ and $d_2$, respectively.  Locations $(c_1, r_1)$ and $(c_2, r_2)$ will never be the same, and $d_1$ and $d_2$ may be different.

After this come two lines which describe the maze.  The first line has the form $n$ $c_1$ $r_1$ $c_2$ $r_2 \ldots c_n$ $r_n$ ($n \leq r*c, 1 \leq c_i \leq c, 1 \leq r_i < r$) where each $c_i$ $r_i$ pair indicates that there is a horizontal wall between grid squares $(c_i,r_i)$ and $(c_i,r_i+1)$.  The next line is similar, where each $c_i$ $r_i$ pair ($n \leq r*c, 1 \leq c_i < c, 1 \leq r_i \leq r$) indicates that there is a vertical wall between grid squares $(c_i,r_i)$ and $(c_i+1,r_i)$.  All exterior walls except the exit are assumed to exist and are not listed in these two lines.

\section*{Output}
Output two values: the minimum number of Forward commands to get the two robots out of the maze and the number of bumps that occur using these commands.  If there are several ways to get the robots out with the same minimum number of Forward command, use the one that minimizes the number of bumps.   It will be possible for both robots to escape the maze in each input instance.

- The final orientation of the waffle depends only on the total rotation angle modulo 360 degrees.
- The waffle must return to horizontal via minimal rotation (less than 90 degrees) after f seconds.
- The blueberry side starts up; its final orientation is determined by the net rotation.
- The total rotation is calculated as (180 * f / r) degrees, and the result depends on whether this is an even or odd multiple of 180 degrees.

2. Count how many full 180-degree rotations occur in f seconds.
3. Determine the final orientation based on whether the number of flips is even or odd.
4. Use modular arithmetic to reduce the total angle to a value in [0, 360) before determining orientation.

SKETCH C: State machine with flip tracking
1. Track the number of 180-degree rotations (flips) that occur in f seconds.
2. If the number of flips is even, the waffle is in the original orientation ("up").
3. If the number of flips is odd, the waffle is flipped ("down").
4. Handle edge cases where the final position is vertical but the machine returns to horizontal via minimal rotation.

SKETCH D: Geometric interpretation with orientation tracking
1. Compute total degrees rotated: θ = (180 * f) / r.
2. Normalize θ to [0, 360) using modulo.
3. If normalized θ is in [0, 180), blueberry side is up.
4. If normalized θ is in [180, 360), blueberry side is down.

```python
r, f = map(int, sys.stdin.readline().split())
```

```python
import sys

def main():
    input = sys.stdin.read
    r, f = map(int, input().split())
    
    # Calculate the total angle rotated

    print(orientation)

if __name__ == "__main__":
    main()
```

Problem:
\problemname{A-Mazing Puzzle}

The Severely Challenging Archaeological Exploration Division (SCArED) specializes in the investigation of archaeological sites that are too difficult, dangerous or downright terrifying for humans to examine firsthand.  Instead they send in one or more mobile robots to investigate, each robot outfitted with a complex set of sensors, recorders and manipulators.  Each robot is radio-controlled and has a small nuclear reactor to power what sometimes ends up being an extended stay at the sites.

Recently a complex underground maze of the Desreverezam Civilization has been discovered.  SCArED sent in two robots to investigate and over the course of several months they have mapped out the entire maze.  But then disaster struck~---~due to some unforeseen anomaly they no longer have radio contact with the two robots (the researchers are torn between the cause of this: either some magnetoferritin crystals in the walls or angry poltergeists).  After several weeks of trying to regain contact with the two robots they finally have managed to open up a shared channel with them.  There's not much bandwidth in the connection, so they can only send three basic commands to the robots: (Move) Forward, (Turn) Left and (Turn) Right.  Since this is a shared channel they cannot send differentiated commands to each robot, so if they send the Forward command each robot will try to move forward simultaneously.  If there is an opening in front of them that's fine but if there's a wall in front of one of the robots it will bump into the wall and remain where it is.   Any Turn command will likewise cause the robots to turn in lockstep.

\begin{figure}[!h]
\centering
\includegraphics[width=0.45\textwidth]{maze}
\caption{Sample Input $1$.}
\label{fig:maze}
\end{figure}

The researchers would like to find the minimum number of Forward commands to get both robots out of the maze (the Turn commands take so little time and power that they can be ignored).  As soon as either is out of the maze they can return to the surface and ignore any future commands.  Consider the situation in Figure \ref{fig:maze} where both robots are currently facing South. One approach to get the robots out of the maze is to first get robot B out as quickly as possible by moving it to locations $(6,2)$, $(6,1)$, $(5,1)$, $(4,1)$ and then out of the maze.  This takes $5$ Forward moves and in the process robot A moves to $(3,1)$ and stays there, bumping against walls $4$ times.  After robot B is out robot A can exit  the maze in $6$ more Forward moves, for a total of $11$ moves.  However, if robot A is moved out as quickly as possible first and then robot B is moved out, only $8$ Forward moves are needed.  For other mazes, the minimum number of Forward moves may not involve getting either robot out as quickly as possible (see Sample Input~2).

Given a maze and the initial positions and orientations of the robots, help the researchers out by determining the minimum number of Forward commands to get both robots out of the maze. A value of 0 represents an empty space. The output should be a sequence of moves, each being a pair of integers $u$ $v$ indicating moving a ball from tube $u$ to tube $v$, with a total number of moves at most $20n$. The goal is to reach a state where each non-empty tube contains 3 balls of the same color or is empty.

INVARIANTS:  
- Each move must be valid: the source tube must have at least one ball and the destination tube must have space (i.e., not full).  
- The number of moves must be at most $20n$.  
- The final configuration must have each tube either empty or filled with 3 balls of the same color.  
- The total number of moves must be sufficient to solve the puzzle, but not exceed the move limit.

The worst-case input size is $n = 1000$, and the target time complexity is polynomial in $n$. Brute-force search is not feasible due to the exponential number of states, but a polynomial-time algorithm is expected to fit within time limits.

SKETCH A: Greedy Ball Matching  
1. Identify all tubes that are not in a solved state (i.e., not monochromatic or empty).  
2. For each unsolved tube, find a target tube that can accept its balls to form a complete color set.  
3. Prioritize moving balls to tubes that are closer to being solved.  
4. Repeat until all tubes are solved or empty.  

SKETCH B: BFS State Space Search  
1. Model the puzzle as a state space where each state is a configuration of tubes.  
2. Use a queue to perform BFS from the initial state to the solved state.  
3. Track the sequence of moves to reach the solution.  
4. Prune paths that exceed the move limit or are already visited.  
5. Output the sequence of moves that leads to a solved state.

1. Use recursive backtracking to explore possible moves.  
2. At each step, select a ball that can be moved to progress toward a solved tube.

4. Backtrack and retry if the current path does not lead to a solution within move limits.

4. Move balls out of high-potential tubes to allow more flexibility in future moves.

Problem:
\problemname{Double Up}

A Double Up game consists of a sequence of $n$ numbers $a_1, \ldots, a_n$,
where each $a_i$ is a power of two. In one move one can either remove one of the numbers,
or merge two identical adjacent numbers into a single number of twice the value.
For example, for sequence $4,2,2,1,8$, we can merge the $2$s and obtain $4,4,1,8$,
then merge the $4$s and obtain $8,1,8$, then remove the $1$, and, finally,
merge the $8$s, obtaining a single final number, $16$. We play the game until a single number remains.
What is the largest number we can obtain?

\section*{Input}
The input consists of two lines. The first line contains $n$ ($1 \leq n \leq 1000$).
The second line contains numbers $a_1, \ldots, a_n$, where $1\leq a_i\leq 2^{100}$ for each $i$.

\section*{Output}
The ouput consists of a single line containing the largest number that can be obtained from the input sequence $a_1, \ldots, a_n$.




Sample Input 1:
5
4 2 2 1 8

Sample Output 1:
16



Your complete Python 3 program:
response_text:
```python
def solve():
    n = int(input())
    a = list(map(int, input().split()))
    
    # We can think of this problem as follows:
    # Each number is a power of 2, so we can represent it by its exponent
    # When we merge two identical numbers (2^k), we get 2^(k+1)
    # The key insight is that we can combine numbers optimally by processing from right to left
    # and keeping track of what we can build
    
    # Convert to exponents for easier handling
    exponents = []
    for num in a:
        # Find the exponent of the power of 2
        exp = 0
        temp = num
        while temp > 1:
            temp //= 2
            exp += 1
        exponents.append(exp)
    
    # Process from right to left
    # We keep track of how many of each exponent we have available
    # When we have 2 of the same exponent, we can combine them into the next higher exponent
    counts = [0] * 200  # Enough to handle up to 2^100
    
    # Process from right to left
    for i in range(n-1, -1, -1):
        exp = exponents[i]
        counts[exp] += 1
        
        # Combine as much as possible
        j = exp
        while counts[j] >= 2:
            counts[j] -= 2
            counts[j + 1] += 1
            j += 1
    
    # Find the highest exponent with count > 0
    max_exp = 0
    for i in range(200):
        if counts[i] > 0:
            max_exp = i
    
    # Calculate the actual value
    result = 1
    for _ in range(max_exp):
        result *= 2
    
    print(result)

solve()
```

SPEC:  
The input consists of an integer $ n $ ($1 \leq n \leq 1000$) followed by a list of $ n $ integers $ a_1, \ldots, a_n $, each a power of two satisfying $1 \leq a_i \leq 2^{100}$. The output is a single integer representing the maximum number obtainable by merging adjacent equal elements and removing others until only one number remains.

INVARIANTS:  
- The final number must be a power of two.  
- The final number cannot exceed the sum of all input numbers.  
- Any number larger than the maximum in the input must be constructible via merges.  
- The process terminates with exactly one number.

COMPLEXITY:  
Worst-case input size: $ n = 1000 $, with each $ a_i $ up to $ 2^{100} $. Target time complexity: polynomial in $ n $, likely $ O(n^2) $ or better. Brute-force simulation of all possibilities is infeasible due to exponential branching; thus brute force does not fit.

SKETCHES:  
SKETCH A: Greedy Right-to-Left Merging  
1. Process the array from right to left.  
2. At each step, if two adjacent elements are equal, merge them.  
3. Otherwise, keep both.  
4. Repeat until one element remains.  
5. This favors creating larger values on the right side.

SKETCH B: Stack-Based Simulation  
1. Use a stack to simulate merging.  
2. For each element in the input, push it onto the stack.  
3. While the top two elements of the stack are equal, merge and push the result.  
4. Continue until all elements are processed.  
5. The final stack top is the answer.

SKETCH C: Dynamic Programming on Subarrays  
1. Define DP[i][j] as the maximum number obtainable from subarray a[i..j].  
2. Base case: DP[i][i] = a[i].  
3. For each interval, try splitting into left and right parts.  
4. If the max values from both parts are equal, merge them.  
5. Otherwise, take the larger of the two.  
6. Compute for all intervals using interval DP.

SKETCH D: Backward Simulation with Bit Tracking  
1. Represent each number as its exponent of 2 (i.e., $ a_i = 2^{b_i} $).  
2. Simulate merging by combining equal adjacent exponents into one higher exponent.  
3. Use a deque or list to simulate the process.  
4. Prioritize merging from the end or high-value side.  
5. Track the maximum exponent seen during the process.

most 3 balls, and each color appears on exactly 3 balls (so there are $n$
colors).

Using a series of moves, you are supposed to reach a Color Tubes
state---each tube should either hold 3 balls of a single color or it
should be empty.

The only move allowed is to take the top ball from one tube and place it into a different tube that has room for it (i.e. holds at most two balls before the move).

You want to write a program to solve this puzzle for you.
Initially, you are not interested in an optimal solution, but you want your program to be good enough to solve any puzzle configuration using at most $20n$ moves.

The first line of input contains a single integer $n$ ($1 \leq n \leq 1\,000$), which is the number of colors.  

Each of the next $n+1$ lines contains three integers $b$, $m$ and $t$ ($0 \le b,m,t \le n$), which are the descriptions of each tube, where $b$ is the color of the ball on the bottom, $m$ is the color of the ball in the middle, and $t$ is the color of the ball on the top.

empty space.  It is guaranteed that no empty space will be below a colored ball.

Problem:
\problemname{Election Paradox}

In Oddland, the leader of the country is determined by a democratic
election.  The country is divided into an odd number of regions, in
which each region has an odd number of voters.

There are two (an even number!) political parties in Oddland, and the
winning party is the one that wins the most number of regions.  A
party wins a region if it receives more votes than the other party in
that region.

Under this system, it is possible that the losing party receives more
votes than the winning party.  For example, if there are three regions
with $11$, $3$, and $3$ people, respectively, then a party could
receive $8$, $1$, and $1$ votes and lose the election.  In this case, the
losing party received the majority of the votes in the total population.

Determine the largest number of votes a party can receive and still lose
the election.


\section*{Input}

The first line of input contains an odd integer $N$ ($3 \leq N \leq
999$), which is the number of regions in Oddland. 

The next line contains $N$ odd integers $p_i$ ($1 \leq p_i \leq 999$),
which are the populations of the $N$ cities.

\section*{Output}

Display the largest number of votes a party can receive and still lose
the election.




Sample Input 1:
3
11 3 3

Sample Output 1:
13

response_text:
```python
n = int(input())
populations = list(map(int, input().split()))

# Sort populations in ascending order
populations.sort()

# To maximize the votes of the losing party while still losing,
# we want the party to win in the smallest regions by the smallest margin (1 vote)
# and lose in the largest regions by the smallest margin (just enough to lose)

# The key insight is:
# - Win in (n-1)//2 regions by smallest margin (1 vote majority)
# - Lose in (n+1)//2 regions by smallest margin (just lose)

# To maximize votes while still losing:
# - Win in the smallest (n-1)//2 regions with just over half votes
# - Lose in the largest (n+1)//2 regions with just under half votes

# But actually, to maximize our total votes while still losing:
# - We should lose in (n+1)/2 regions (which is the majority of regions)
# - We should win in (n-1)/2 regions
# - To maximize total votes, we should:
#   * Win the smallest (n-1)//2 regions by smallest possible majorities
#   * Lose the largest (n+1)//2 regions by smallest margins

# The optimal strategy:
# - Win in the first (n-1)//2 smallest regions with just enough votes to win
# - Lose in the remaining (n+1)//2 regions (largest ones) with as many votes as possible while still losing

# Sort regions by population
# Win in smallest regions: need (population//2 + 1) votes to win
# Lose in largest regions: get (population//2) votes (just lose)

# But wait, we want to MAXIMIZE our total votes while still losing the election
# So we should lose in the (n+1)//2 regions (the majority needed to win the election)
# and win in the remaining (n-1)//2 regions.

# Strategy:
# - Lose in the (n+1)//2 LARGEST regions (get as many votes as possible in these)
# - Win in the (n-1)//2 SMALLEST regions (get minimal winning votes)

# Number of regions to win = (n-1)//2 (we must lose overall, so we win less than half)
# Number of regions to lose = (n+1)//2 (this is the majority)

# To maximize our votes while still losing:
# - Win the smallest (n-1)//2 regions with minimal majorities
# - Lose the largest (n+1)//2 regions but get maximum votes in them

# Sort populations
populations.sort()

# We need to win in (n-1)//2 regions to lose overall
# To maximize our total votes while losing, we should:
# - Win in the smallest (n-1)//2 regions with minimal majorities: population//2 + 1 votes
# - Lose in the remaining (n+1)//2 regions but get as many votes as possible: population//2 votes

votes = 0
half_regions = (n - 1) // 2  # Number of regions we win

# Win in the smallest regions
for i in range(half_regions):
    votes += populations[i] // 2 + 1

# Lose in the remaining regions (but get maximum votes while losing)
for i in range(half_regions, n):
    votes += populations[i] // 2

print(votes)
```

A Double Up game consists of a sequence of $n$ numbers $a_1, \ldots, a_n$,
where each $a_i$ is a power of two. In one move one can either remove one of the numbers,

then merge the $4$s and obtain $8,1,8$, then remove the $1$, and, finally,
merge the $8$s, obtaining a single final number, $16$. We play the game until a single number remains.
What is the largest number we can obtain?

\section*{Input}
The input consists of two lines. The first line contains $n$ ($1 \leq n \leq 1000$).
The second line contains numbers $a_1, \ldots, a_n$, where $1\leq a_i\leq 2^{100}$ for each $i$.

```python
import sys
import math

def main():
    import sys
    import math

    # Read input
    input_lines = []
    for line in sys.stdin:
        input_lines.append(line.strip())
    
    n = int(input_lines[0])
    a = list(map(int, input_lines[1].split()))

    # Precompute logarithm base 2 for quick comparison
    log_a = [int(math.log2(x)) for x in a]

    # dp[i][j] will store the maximum value obtainable from subarray a[i..j]
    # We'll use logarithm values to avoid huge numbers
    dp = [[-1 for _ in range(n)] for __ in range(n)]
    # To store actual values if needed, but we can work with logs
    # Since all numbers are powers of 2, we can work with exponents
    # dp_val[i][j] stores the exponent of 2 for the max value from a[i..j]
    dp_val = [[-1 for _ in range(n)] for __ in range(n)]

    # Base case: dp[i][i] = a[i]
    for i in range(n):
        dp_val[i][i] = log_a[i]

    # Fill DP table for intervals of increasing length
    for length in range(2, n+1):  # length of the interval
        for i in range(n - length + 1):
            j = i + length - 1
            # Initialize with the value of the first element (or just set to -1)
            dp_val[i][j] = -1
            # Try all possible splits
            for k in range(i, j):
                left = dp_val[i][k]
                right = dp_val[k+1][j]
                
                # If we have valid values from both sides
                if left != -1 and right != -1:
                    if left == right:
                        # Merge: 2^left + 2^right = 2^(left+1)
                        merged = left + 1
                        if merged > dp_val[i][j]:
                            dp_val[i][j] = merged
                    else:
                        # Take the max of the two
                        max_part = max(left, right)
                        if max_part > dp_val[i][j]:
                            dp_val[i][j] = max_part
                elif left != -1:
                    if left > dp_val[i][j]:
                        dp_val[i][j] = left
                elif right != -1:
                    if right > dp_val[i][j]:
                        dp_val[i][j] = right

    # The answer is 2^(dp_val[0][n-1])
    print(2 ** dp_val[0][n-1])

if __name__ == "__main__":
    main()
```

```python
import sys
from collections import deque

def main():
    input = sys.stdin.read
    data = input().split()
    
    n = int(data[0])
    a = list(map(int, data[1:]))
    
    # Convert each number to its exponent of 2
    b = []
    for x in a:
        exponent = 0
        while x > 1:
            x //= 2
            exponent += 1
        b.append(exponent)
    
    # Use a deque to simulate the process
    dq = deque(b)
    max_exponent = 0
    
    # Process until only one element remains
    while len(dq) > 1:
        # Prioritize merging from the end (right side)
        # Check if last two elements are equal
        if len(dq) >= 2 and dq[-1] == dq[-2]:
            # Merge them
            exponent = dq.pop() + 1
            dq.pop()
            dq.append(exponent)
            max_exponent = max(max_exponent, exponent)
        else:
            # If we can't merge from the end, try from the front
            if len(dq) >= 2 and dq[0] == dq[1]:
                exponent = dq.popleft() + 1
                dq.popleft()
                dq.appendleft(exponent)
                max_exponent = max(max_exponent, exponent)
            else:
                # If no adjacent elements can be merged, remove the smallest element
                # But to maximize the final result, we should be strategic
                # Let's find the rightmost pair that can be merged
                merged = False
                # Iterate from right to left to find a mergeable pair
                for i in range(len(dq) - 2, -1, -1):
                    if dq[i] == dq[i+1]:
                        # Merge these two
                        exponent = dq[i] + 1
                        dq[i] = exponent
                        del dq[i+1]
                        max_exponent = max(max_exponent, exponent)
                        merged = True
                        break
                if not merged:
                    # If no merge is possible, remove an element to enable future merges
                    # Remove the leftmost element to preserve higher-value elements on the right
                    dq.popleft()
    
    # The remaining element is the maximum exponent
    final_exponent = dq[0]
    max_exponent = max(max_exponent, final_exponent)
    
    # Convert back to the actual number
    result = 2 ** max_exponent
    print(result)

if __name__ == "__main__":
    main()
```

SPEC:  
Input consists of an odd integer \( N \) (where \( 3 \leq N \leq 999 \)), followed by a list of \( N \) odd integers \( p_i \) (each \( 1 \leq p_i \leq 999 \)), representing the populations of the regions.  
Output is a single integer: the maximum number of votes a party can receive and still lose the election.

INVARIANTS:  
- The total number of regions \( N \) is odd.  
- Each region population \( p_i \) is odd.  
- A party loses the election if it wins fewer than \( \frac{N+1}{2} \) regions.  
- The answer must be achievable under valid vote distributions respecting each region's population.

COMPLEXITY:  
Worst-case input size: \( N = 999 \), with each \( p_i = 999 \).  
Target time complexity: Polynomial (likely \( O(N \log N) \) or better).  
Brute-force search of all vote distributions is infeasible due to exponential combinations; hence brute force does NOT fit.

SKETCHES:  
SKETCH A: Greedy Region Assignment  
1. Sort regions by population in ascending order.  
2. Assign just enough votes to win the smallest \( (N-1)/2 \) regions.  
3. Assign maximal possible votes to the remaining regions without winning them.  
4. Sum up the votes from both winning and losing regions to compute total.  

SKETCH B: Min-Cut Based Optimization  
1. Model the problem as a selection of regions to lose in order to maximize votes.  
2. Use a min-heap or sorting to select regions where losing gives maximum vote return.  
3. For \( (N+1)/2 \) regions, assign minimal winning votes; for others, assign maximal losing votes.  
4. Compute and return the total votes from all regions under this configuration.  

SKETCH C: Mathematical Optimization via Median  
1. Recognize that to lose with maximum votes, we must win the least expensive \( (N-1)/2 \) regions.  
2. Sort regions by population.  
3. For the first \( (N-1)/2 \) regions, assign \( \lceil p_i/2 \rceil \) votes to win.  
4. For the remaining regions, assign \( \lfloor p_i/2 \rfloor \) votes to lose.  
5. Sum all assigned votes to get the result.  

SKETCH D: Dynamic Programming with Thresholds  
1. Define subproblem: maximum votes obtainable while losing in exactly \( k \) regions.  
2. Use DP table where \( dp[i][k] \) = max votes using first \( i \) regions and losing in \( k \).  
3. Transition by either winning or losing each region, updating vote totals accordingly.  
4. Final answer is \( \max_{k > N/2} dp[N][k] \).  
(Note: This approach is likely too slow but conceptually distinct.)

```python
import sys

def main():

    input = sys.stdin.read
    data = input().split()
    
    N = int(data[0])
    populations = list(map(int, data[1:]))
    
    # Sort regions by population in ascending order

    # To lose the election, you must win less than (N+1)/2 regions, i.e., at most (N-1)/2 regions
    num_win_regions = (N - 1) // 2

    for i in range(num_win_regions, N):
```

```python
import sys
import math

# Read input
input_lines = []
for line in sys.stdin:
    input_lines.append(line.strip())

N = int(input_lines[0])
populations = list(map(int, input_lines[1].split()))

# Sort the populations
sorted_populations = sorted(populations)

# Calculate number of regions to win (least (N-1)//2 regions)
num_win = (N - 1) // 2

# For the first num_win regions, we need to win, so we take ceil(p_i / 2)
# For the remaining regions, we lose, so we take floor(p_i / 2)
total_votes = 0

# Win the smallest (N-1)//2 regions with minimum cost
for i in range(num_win):
    p = sorted_populations[i]
    votes_to_win = math.ceil(p / 2)
    total_votes += votes_to_win

# Lose in the remaining (N+1)//2 regions, take floor(p_i / 2)
for i in range(num_win, N):
    p = sorted_populations[i]
    votes_to_lose = math.floor(p / 2)
    total_votes += votes_to_lose

print(total_votes)
```

```python
        # Accumulate mess
        accum_mess = [0] * n
        accum_mess[0] = mess_made[0]
        for i in range(1, n):
            accum_mess[i] = accum_mess[i-1] + mess_made[i]
        
        # For each visit, we must ensure all mess up to that point is cleaned
        # We'll use a greedy approach: clean as late as possible
        must_clean = [0] * n  # How much we must clean on day i
        
        prev_visit = -1
        for visit_day in visits:
            # Check mess accumulated from start or last visit
            total_mess = accum_mess[visit_day]
            # Already cleaned
            already_cleaned = sum(must_clean[i] for i in range(visit_day + 1))
            
            if already_cleaned < total_mess:
                needed_cleaning = total_mess - already_cleaned
                # Distribute this cleaning from visit_day backwards
                remaining = needed_cleaning
                for day in range(visit_day, -1, -1):
                    if remaining <= 0:
                        break
                    already_allocated = must_clean[day]
                    available = clean_cap[day] - already_allocated
                    if available > 0:
                        to_clean = min(available, remaining)
                        must_clean[day] += to_clean
                        remaining -= to_clean
        
        # Count days we actually clean
        count = 0
        for i in range(n):
            if must_clean[i] > 0:
                count += 1
                
        # Verify solution is valid
        mess = 0
        for i in range(n):
            mess += mess_made[i]
            mess -= must_clean[i]
            if mess < 0:
                mess = 0
            if i in visits and mess > 0:
                return -1
        return count
    
    result = min_cleaning_days()
    print(result)

solve()
```

\problemname{Family Visits}

You are a college student living on your own.
However, your doting family still likes to visit you, and
they often stop by to check on your room at night before going to dinner.
Your family will be worried if they find a mess in your room.
Therefore you make an effort to ensure that they never see a mess in your room when visiting at night.
You have some free time each afternoon that allows you to clean up,
but the amount of free time varies each day due to prior commitments.

Luckily, your schedule is planned out well.
You know exactly how big of a mess you will make each morning, 
how much mess you can clean each afternoon, 
and on which nights your family will stop by. 
Since you are lazy, you want to spend as few afternoons as possible cleaning such that your family will always see a room without any mess. 
You may assume that your room starts completely clean, and any mess that is not cleaned remains until it is cleaned.

\section*{Input}

The first line of input contains two integers, $n$ and $d$ ($1 \leq d \leq n \leq 1\,000$), where $n$ is the number of days
in your schedule and $d$ is the number of days your family will visit. 

Each of the next $n$ lines contains two integers $m$ and $c$ ($0 \le m,c \le 1\,000$). For each day, in order, $m$ is the amount of mess you make in the morning, and $c$ is the amount you can clean in the afternoon.

Each of the next $d$ lines contains a single integer $v$ ($1 \le v \le n$). These are the days on which your family will visit, and they are listed in strictly increasing order.  


\section*{Output}

Output the smallest number of afternoons you have to spend cleaning
to ensure your family will never see a mess.  
If it is not possible, output $-1$.

The next $ n $ lines each contain two integers $ m $ and $ c $ ($ 0 \leq m, c \leq 1000 $), representing the mess made in the morning and the cleaning capacity in the afternoon for each day. The next $ d $ lines each contain a single integer $ v $ ($ 1 \leq v \leq n $), indicating the 1-indexed days on which the family will visit, in strictly increasing order. The output is a single integer: the minimum number of afternoons spent cleaning to ensure the room is clean on all family visit days, or -1 if it's impossible.

- The total mess accumulated before each family visit must be less than or equal to the total cleaning done up to that point.  
- Cleaning is only performed in the afternoon, and any leftover mess remains until cleaned.  
- The number of afternoons used must be minimized while ensuring no mess is visible during visits.

3. Use a greedy selection of cleaning days to meet the visit constraints.  
4. Check feasibility by simulating forward and ensuring no mess is visible on visit days.  

SKETCH D: Dynamic Programming on Visit Days  
1. Process the problem in segments between visit days.  
2. For each segment, compute the minimal cleaning afternoons required to reduce accumulated mess to zero.  
3. Use DP to track minimal cleaning afternoons needed up to each visit day.

```python
import sys

def can_clean_in_days(n, d, mess_clean, visit_days, max_clean_days):
    # We are allowed to clean on at most max_clean_days afternoons
    # Try to simulate whether it's possible to keep room clean on all visit days

    # clean_capacity[i] = cleaning capacity on day i (0-indexed)

    # Keep track of mess level

    # Simulate forward, keep track of mess
    # When we see a visit day and mess > 0, we must have cleaned on some previous days
    # We can clean on any previous day where c > 0 and we didn't clean yet
    # We want to minimize cleaning days, so we should clean on days with highest c
    
    # So we simulate forward:
    # Keep a list of days we can clean (c > 0) and haven't decided to clean yet
    # When we must be clean (visit day), check if we are
    # If not, we must clean on some previous days
    
    # Let's do this:
    # Keep a list of potential cleaning days (days with c > 0), with their capacities
    # When we must be clean, and we are not, we clean from the highest capacity days
    
    # Actually, let's do a greedy simulation:
    # We'll simulate day by day:
    #   - Add mess
    #   - If it's a visit day, check if we are clean
    #   - If not, we must have cleaned on some previous days
    #   - We can clean on any day with c > 0 that we haven't cleaned yet
    #   - We want to minimize total cleaning days, so we should clean on days with highest c
    
    # So we can:
    # - Keep a list of available cleaning days (c > 0) that we haven't used yet
    # - When we must be clean, and we are not, we clean on the best days
    
    # Let's implement this:
    
    current_mess = 0
    # List of (capacity, day_index) for days we can clean (c > 0)
    available_cleaning = []
    cleaned_on = [False] * n  # Which days we decided to clean on
    
    visit_set = set(v - 1 for v in visit_days)  # Convert to 0-indexed
    
    for day in range(n):
        m, c = mess_clean[day]
        current_mess += m  # Add morning mess
        
        # If this is a visit day, we must be clean
        if day in visit_set:
            if current_mess > 0:
                # We must clean some previous days to get to 0
                # We can clean on any day where c > 0 and we haven't cleaned yet
                # We want to use as few cleaning days as possible
                # So we should clean on days with highest c
                
                # Add current day to available cleaning if c > 0 and not already cleaned
                if c > 0 and not cleaned_on[day]:
                    available_cleaning.append((c, day))
                
                # We need to reduce current_mess to 0
                # Sort available_cleaning by capacity descending
                available_cleaning.sort(reverse=True)
                
                # Clean until current_mess <= 0
                i = 0
                while current_mess > 0 and i < len(available_cleaning):

                    if not cleaned_on[clean_day]:

                        if cleaning_days_used > max_clean_days:

                        current_mess -= cap
                    i += 1

            else:

        else:
            # Not a visit day, but we can clean if needed later
            if c > 0 and not cleaned_on[day]:
                available_cleaning.append((c, day))
```

PROBLEM (statement):
\problemname{Food Processor}

You have a food processor with a variety of blades that can be attached to it, as well as some food you would like to process into smaller pieces.

The food processor can have one blade attached at any time. 
Each blade processes food by reducing its average piece size at a particular exponential rate, but it also has a maximum average piece size requirement; 
if the average piece size of the food is too big for the blade, the food processor will get stuck. Given a starting average food piece size, a target average piece size, 
and a set of blades for your food processor, determine the minimum amount of processing time needed to process your food into the target average piece size. 

Note that we only care about the time spent actively processing food; we do not track time spent switching out blades or loading/unloading the food processor.

\section*{Input}
The first line of input contains three integers $s$, $t$, and $n$ ($1 \le t < s \le 10^6, 1 \le n \le 10^5$), where
$s$ is the starting average piece size, $t$ is the target average
piece size, and $n$ is the number of blades.

Each of the next $n$ lines contains two integers $m$ and $h$ ($1 \le m, h \le 10^6$). These are the
blades, where $m$ is the maximum average piece size of the blade and
$h$ is the number of seconds the blade needs to halve the average
piece size.

\section*{Output}

Output a single number, which is the minimum amount of time in
seconds needed to process the food to the target average piece size.
If it is not possible to reach the target, output $-1$. Your answer
should have a \textit{relative} error of at most $10^{-5}$.


PUBLIC SAMPLES:
--- sample 1 ---
INPUT:
10 1 2
10 10
4 5

OUTPUT:
23.219281

--- sample 2 ---
INPUT:
10000 9999 1
10000 1

OUTPUT:
1.4427671804501932E-4
response_text:
SPEC:
- Input: Three integers `s`, `t`, `n` on the first line, where `s` is the starting average piece size, `t` is the target average piece size, and `n` is the number of blades. Then `n` lines follow, each containing two integers `m` and `h`, representing a blade with max average piece size `m` and halving time `h`.
- Constraints: $1 \le t < s \le 10^6$, $1 \le n \le 10^5$, $1 \le m, h \le 10^6$.
- Output: A single real number representing the minimum time to reach the target average piece size, or `-1` if impossible. Relative error must be at most $10^{-5}$.

INVARIANTS:
- The total processing time is the sum of time spent using each blade.
- Each blade can only be used when the current average piece size is at most its max average piece size.
- Time spent on a blade is calculated using the logarithmic formula based on halving time.
- If no valid sequence of blades can reduce the size to the target, output is `-1`.

COMPLEXITY:
- Worst-case input size: $n = 10^5$, with $s, t, m, h \leq 10^6$.
- Target time complexity: Likely $O(n \log n)$ or better due to optimization over blade selection.
- Brute force (checking all sequences) is infeasible due to exponential combinations.

SKETCHES:
SKETCH A: Greedy Selection with Priority Queue
1. Model each blade's effectiveness as a rate of reduction per unit time.
2. Use a priority queue to always select the most effective blade available.
3. Simulate processing by applying the best blade until the size drops below its max.
4. Repeat until reaching the target or determining it's impossible.

SKETCH B: Dynamic Programming over Blade States
1. Define DP states as (current_size, time) and transition using each blade.
2. For each blade, compute the time to process from current size to the blade's max size.
3. Use memoization to avoid recomputation of subproblems.
4. Track minimum time to reach the target size across all valid paths.

SKETCH C: Binary Search on Time with Feasibility Check
1. Binary search over possible total time values.
2. For a given time, simulate whether it's possible to reach the target.
3. Use greedy or priority-based blade selection within the time limit.
4. Check feasibility by processing as much as possible within the time budget.

SKETCH D: Mathematical Modeling with Continuous Optimization
1. Treat blade usage as continuous variables in a minimization problem.
2. Express total time as a sum of logarithmic functions based on blade halving times.
3. Use calculus or optimization techniques to find the minimum time configuration.
4. Discretize the solution back to valid blade usage steps for output.

Problem:
\problemname{Hilbert's Hedge Maze}

\begin{minipage}{6.5in}
\illustration{0.3}{hilbert.png}{{\footnotesize The shortest path from the first case in the sample input.}}

David has elaborate hedge mazes in his garden. For each of his mazes of different sizes, he wonders how long it would 
take to walk between any pair of locations in and around that maze. 
A hedge maze of order $n\geq 1$ is constructed according to very specific instructions:

\begin{enumerate}
	\item Start with $S=A$.
\item Repeat $n$ times:
\begin{enumerate}
	\item Create a new string $T$ by replacing each $A$ in $S$ with $LBFRAFARFBL$ and each $B$ with $RAFLBFBLFAR$.
    \item Set $S=T$.
\end{enumerate}
\item Remove all $A$'s and all $B$'s from $S$.
\end{enumerate}
\end{minipage}
\medskip

The resulting string gives the instructions for constructing the hedge maze. In the unbounded 
plane start at coordinates $(0,0)$, facing towards $(1,0)$ and for every $F$ move forward 
one unit, for every $R$ turn right $90$ degrees, and for every $L$ turn left $90$ degrees.


After constructing the maze, identify position $(x,y)$ with the unit square cell
bounded by coordinates $(x,y),(x+1,y),(x,y+1),(x+1,y+1)$. We can move directly 
between two positions $(x_1,y_1)$, $(x_2,y_2)$ if and only if either $|x_1-x_2|=1$ or 
$|y_1-y_2|=1$, but not both, and there is no hedge between these cells. The distance 
between these two positions is $1$.

Given a number $n$ and a pair of positions, find the shortest distance between
these positions.

Input starts with a positive integer $k$ $(1\leq k\leq 100)$,

There is no interaction between the test cases. Each specifies a single hedge maze
situated alone in the unbounded plane.

\section*{Output}

Output $k$ lines with one integer on each line, corresponding to the shortest distance 
between the two positions in each of the $k$ test cases.

```python
    # x[i] is the time spent using blade i

    current_size = s

            current_size = current_size * factor

    # We will use a continuous relaxation:
    # Let x_i be the time spent using blade i
    # The size evolves as: s_i = s_{i-1} * 2^(-x_i / h_i)
    # We want to minimize sum(x_i) such that final size <= t

    # We need to decide the order of using blades
    # A greedy approach: sort blades by max size descending
    # But for optimization, we can let the optimizer decide

    # Let's assume we use blades in the order they are given
    # and let the optimizer choose the times

    n = len(blades)

    # Initial guess: equal time on each usable blade
    x0 = [1.0] * n

    # Bounds: time >= 0
    bounds = [(0, None) for _ in range(n)]

    # Constraints
    cons = {'type': 'ineq', 'fun': lambda x: constraint_final_size(x, s, t, blades)}

    result = minimize(objective, x0, method='SLSQP', bounds=bounds, constraints=cons, options={'maxiter': 1000})

        # Use only one blade

                if s <= m:

                    min_time = min(min_time, time_needed)
        if min_time < float('inf'):
            return min_time
        return -1

def main():

    result = solve(s, t, blades)
    if result == -1:
        print(-1)
    else:
        print(result)
```

```python
import sys
from collections import deque, defaultdict

# Precompute the Hilbert curve instructions for all n up to 50
def generate_hilbert_string(n):
    if n in hilbert_strings:
        return hilbert_strings[n]
    S = 'A'
    for _ in range(n):
        T = ''
        for c in S:
            if c == 'A':
                T += 'LBFRAFARFBL'
            elif c == 'B':
                T += 'RAFLBFBLFAR'
            else:
                T += c
        S = T
    # Remove A and B
    result = ''.join(c for c in S if c not in 'AB')
    hilbert_strings[n] = result
    return result

# Simulate the Hilbert curve to build a grid representation
def simulate_hilbert_curve(n):
    path = generate_hilbert_string(n)
    # Directions: 0=right, 1=down, 2=left, 3=up
    dx = [1, 0, -1, 0]
    dy = [0, -1, 0, 1]
    x, y, direction = 0, 0, 0
    positions = [(0, 0)]
    for move in path:
        if move == 'F':
            x += dx[direction]
            y += dy[direction]
            positions.append((x, y))
        elif move == 'L':
            direction = (direction - 1) % 4
        elif move == 'R':
            direction = (direction + 1) % 4
    return positions

# Build a graph of connected components from the path
def build_graph(positions):
    graph = defaultdict(set)
    for i in range(len(positions) - 1):
        x1, y1 = positions[i]
        x2, y2 = positions[i+1]
        if abs(x1 - x2) + abs(y1 - y2) == 1:  # adjacent
            graph[(x1, y1)].add((x2, y2))
            graph[(x2, y2)].add((x1, y1))
    return graph

# BFS to find shortest path in the graph
def bfs(graph, start, end, max_distance):
    if start == end:
        return 0
    queue = deque([(start, 0)])
    visited = {start}
    while queue:
        (x, y), dist = queue.popleft()
        if dist >= max_distance:
            continue
        for nx, ny in graph.get((x, y), []):
            if (nx, ny) == end:
                return dist + 1
            if (nx, ny) not in visited:
                visited.add((nx, ny))
                queue.append(((nx, ny), dist + 1))
    return -1  # Should not happen if connected

# Main function to process all test cases
def main():
    import sys
    input_lines = sys.stdin.read().strip().split('\n')
    k = int(input_lines[0])
    results = []
    # Precompute all required Hilbert strings

    max_n = 0

    for i in range(1, k+1):

        n, x1, y1, x2, y2 = parts
        max_n = max(max_n, n)
        test_cases.append((n, x1, y1, x2, y2))
    
    # Precompute all necessary Hilbert curves up to max_n
    graphs = {}
    for n in range(1, max_n + 1):
        positions = simulate_hilbert_curve(n)
        graphs[n] = build_graph(positions)
    
    for n, x1, y1, x2, y2 in test_cases:
        start = (x1, y1)
        end = (x2, y2)
        # We use a large enough BFS depth to find the path
        # Since coordinates can be huge, we simulate only the relative structure
        # But for BFS, we need to work within the graph

        distance = bfs(graph, start, end, 1000000)  # Large enough limit
        if distance == -1:
            # Fallback: use the path length from simulation if not found
            # But in a proper implementation, we should map large coordinates to the finite graph

            results.append(-1)
        else:

    # Since we have to handle large coordinates, we need a smarter mapping
```

```python
import sys
from collections import defaultdict

def main():
    import sys
    input = sys.stdin.read
    data = input().split()
    N = int(data[0])
    a = list(map(int, data[1:1+N]))
    b = list(map(int, data[1+N:1+2*N]))
    
    # Precompute prefix sums of frequencies
    prefix_a = defaultdict(int)
    prefix_b = defaultdict(int)
    freq_a = defaultdict(int)
    freq_b = defaultdict(int)
    
    # Populate frequency counts
    for i in range(N):
        freq_a[a[i]] += 1
        freq_b[b[i]] += 1
    
    # Compute prefix sums
    prefix_freq_a = [dict() for _ in range(N)]
    prefix_freq_b = [dict() for _ in range(N)]
    pa = defaultdict(int)
    pb = defaultdict(int)
    for i in range(N):
        pa[a[i]] += 1
        pb[b[i]] += 1
        prefix_freq_a[i] = pa.copy()
        prefix_freq_b[i] = pb.copy()
        # Subtract 1 because of 0-based indexing in list terms
        prefix_freq_a[i] = {k: v for k, v in pa.items()}
        prefix_freq_b[i] = {k: v for k, v in pb.items()}
    
    # Reset the dictionaries for actual prefix use
    for k in freq_a:
        prefix_freq_a = [{k: v for k, v in freq_a.items()}]
        prefix_freq_b = [{k: v for k, v in freq_b.items()}]
    
    # Compute where the prefix sums match
    groups = []
    i = 0
    current = defaultdict(int)
    current_b = defaultdict(int)
    for idx in range(N):
        # Update current frequency maps
        for k, v in freq_a.items():
            current[k] += v
        for k in list(freq_a.keys()):
            if current[k] == prefix_freq_b[idx][k]:
                groups.append(i)
                i = idx + 1
        # If at any index, the prefix frequency of a and b match, we can place a divider
        if prefix_freq_a[idx] == prefix_freq_b[idx]:
            groups.append(idx)
    # If we have only one such group, no # needed at the start
    dividers = [False] * N
    for idx in groups:
        dividers[idx] = True

    # Now build the output list with # inserted
    result = []
    for i in range(N):
        if i > 0 and dividers[i-1]:
            result.append("#")
        result.append(str(b[i]))
    if dividers[N-1]:
        result.append("#")
    print(' '.join(result))

if __name__ == "__main__":
    main()
```

```python
import sys
from collections import defaultdict

def compute_prefix_hashes(arr, base=1000000007, mod=2**61-1):
    n = len(arr)
    prefix_hash = [0] * (n + 1)
    pow_base = [1] * (n + 1)
    for i in range(n):
        prefix_hash[i+1] = (prefix_hash[i] * base + arr[i]) % mod
        pow_base[i+1] = (pow_base[i] * base) % mod
    return prefix_hash, pow_base, mod

def get_hash(prefix_hash, pow_base, mod, l, r):
    # returns hash of arr[l..r] (0-based inclusive)
    hash_val = (prefix_hash[r+1] - prefix_hash[l] * pow_base[r-l+1]) % mod
    return hash_val

def main():
    input = sys.stdin.read
    data = input().split()
    N = int(data[0])
    a = list(map(int, data[1:N+1]))
    b = list(map(int, data[N+1:2*N+1]))

    # Map each unique value to a unique integer for hashing
    unique_vals = list(set(a + b))
    val_to_int = {val: i+1 for i, val in enumerate(unique_vals)}
    a_int = [val_to_int[x] for x in a]
    b_int = [val_to_int[x] for x in b]

    # Compute prefix hashes for both sequences
    ha, pow_a, moda = compute_prefix_hashes(a_int)
    hb, pow_b, modb = compute_prefix_hashes(b_int)

    # Function to check if subarrays a[l..r] and b[l..r] are rearrangements
    def is_rearrangement(l, r):
        hash_a = get_hash(ha, pow_a, moda, l, r)
        hash_b = get_hash(hb, pow_b, modb, l, r)
        return hash_a == hash_b

    # Greedily find the maximum number of groups
    result = []
    i = 0
    last_cut = 0
    while i < N:
        # Try to make a cut after position i
        if is_rearrangement(last_cut, i):
            # All elements from last_cut to i form a group
            result.append(i)
            last_cut = i + 1
        i += 1

    # Build output string
    output_parts = []
    for j in range(N):
        output_parts.append(str(b[j]))
        if j in result and j != N-1:
            output_parts.append('#')
    print(' '.join(output_parts))

if __name__ == "__main__":
    main()
```

```python
import sys
from collections import defaultdict

def read_input():
    import sys
    input = sys.stdin.read
    data = input().split()
    N = int(data[0])
    a = list(map(int, data[1:N+1]))
    b = list(map(int, data[N+1:2*N+1]))
    return N, a, b

def main():
    N, a, b = read_input()
    
    # Step 1: Pair indices of elements in a and b by value
    # For each value, store the list of indices where it appears in a and b
    a_index = defaultdict(list)
    b_index = defaultdict(list)
    
    for i in range(N):
        a_index[a[i]].append(i)
        b_index[b[i]].append(i)
    
    # Step 2: For each value, pair the indices in a and b
    # We need to sort the indices of b according to the order of indices in a
    # For that, we create a mapping from b's index to a's index for the same value
    b_to_a_order = [0] * N
    for value in a_index:
        a_indices = a_index[value]
        b_indices = b_index[value]
        b_indices_sorted = sorted(b_indices)
        a_indices_sorted = sorted(a_indices)
        for i in range(len(b_indices_sorted)):
            b_to_a_order[b_indices_sorted[i]] = a_indices_sorted[i]
    
    # Step 3: Identify breakpoints where the indices align to form valid segments
    # We use a cumulative count approach to find valid breakpoints
    # For each position, we count how many elements up to that position match in multiset
    # If at position i, the count of each element in a[0..i] equals that in b[0..i], then i+1 is a valid breakpoint
    
    # We'll use a dictionary to track the difference in counts
    count_diff = defaultdict(int)
    breakpoints = [False] * N
    
    for i in range(N):
        # For a[i], increment its count
        count_diff[a[i]] += 1
        # For b[i], decrement its count
        count_diff[b[i]] -= 1
        
        # If all counts are zero, then up to this index is a valid segment
        # We check if all values in count_diff are zero
        # Since we only track non-zero counts, if dict is empty, all counts are zero
        if all(v == 0 for v in count_diff.values()):
            breakpoints[i] = True
    
    # Step 4: Output the sequence b with '#' inserted at breakpoints
    result = []
    for i in range(N):
        if i > 0 and breakpoints[i-1]:
            result.append('#')
        result.append(str(b[i]))
    
    print(' '.join(result))

if __name__ == "__main__":
    main()
```

```python
import sys
from collections import defaultdict

def compute_suffix_array_and_lcp(s, max_value=10**9 + 1):
    n = len(s)
    # Create tuples of (value, index) and sort by value to get the order
    indexed = [(s[i], i) for i in range(n)]
    indexed.sort()
    index_map = {idx: i for i, (_, idx) in enumerate(indexed)}
    
    # Create the suffix array SA and inverse SA
    sa = [idx for _, idx in indexed]
    inv_sa = [0] * n
    for i in range(n):
        inv_sa[sa[i]] = i

    # Compute LCP array
    lcp = [0] * (n - 1)
    for i in range(n - 1):
        # Compare suffixes starting at sa[i] and sa[i+1]
        l = 0
        while sa[i] + l < n and sa[i+1] + l < n and s[sa[i] + l] == s[sa[i+1] + l]:
            l += 1
        lcp[i] = l

    return sa, inv_sa, lcp

def main():

    input = sys.stdin.read
    data = input().split()

        pos_b[b[i]].append(i)

    # We'll use this to check if a segment is a rearrangement
    def build_prefix_count(arr):
        n = len(arr)

        count = defaultdict(int)
        for i in range(n):
            count[arr[i]] += 1
            for k, v in count.items():
                prefix_count[i+1][k] = v
        return prefix_count

    prefix_count_a = build_prefix_count(a)
    prefix_count_b = build_prefix_count(b)

    # Check for all positions where we can make a cut
    # A cut can be made after position i if the multiset of a[0..i] equals that of b[0..i]
    valid_cuts = [False] * N
    count_a = defaultdict(int)
    count_b = defaultdict(int)

        count_b[b[i]] += 1
        if count_a == count_b:
            valid_cuts[i] = True

    # Now we want to place the maximum number of dividers

    result = []
    for i in range(N):

        if i < N - 1 and valid_cuts[i]:
            result.append('#')
    
    print(' '.join(result))
```

Problem:
\problemname{Slide Count}

In your programming class, you are given an assignment to analyze an
integer array using a sliding window algorithm.  Specifically, given
$N$ integers $w_1, \ldots, w_N$ and some constant $C$, the sliding
window algorithm maintains start and end indices $s$ and $e$ such that
\begin{itemize}
\item initially $s = e  = 1$;
\item as long as $s \leq N$:
  \begin{itemize}
  \item if $e+1 > N$, then increment $s$;
  \item else if $w_s + \cdots + w_{e+1} > C$, then
    increment $s$;
  \item else increment $e$.
  \end{itemize}
\end{itemize}
During the execution of this algorithm, each distinct pair of indices
$(s,e)$ defines a window.  An element $w_i$ belongs to the window
defined by $(s,e)$ if $s \leq i \leq e$.  Notice that if $s > e$, the
window is empty.

Consider the first sample input below.  The windows appearing
during the execution of the algorithm are defined by $(1,1)$, $(1,2)$,
$(1,3)$, $(2,3)$, $(3,3)$, $(3,4)$, $(4,4)$, $(5,4)$, $(5,5)$, and
$(6,5)$.

For each element $w_i$, determine how many different windows it
belongs to during the execution of the sliding window algorithm.

\section*{Input}

The first line of input contains two integers $N$
($1 \leq N \leq 100\,000$), which is the number of elements, and $C$
($1 \leq C \leq 1\,000\,000$), which is the sliding window constant. 

The next line contains $N$ integers $w_1, \ldots, w_N$
